title: Volunteering at the Animal Shelter
summary: A Sydney narrator describes her Saturday mornings volunteering at a local animal shelter and the quiet old dog who slowly came back to life.
body: I started volunteering at a small animal shelter near Sydney harbour about eight months ago. I go every Saturday morning, from eight until noon. There are usually three or four other volunteers, and we share the work. I am not strong enough to lift the big bags of food, but I can walk dogs, clean the cat room, and fold the small towels for the puppies. The first time, I was a little afraid because some dogs barked very loudly. After a few weeks, I learned that loud dogs are often the most scared, not the most dangerous. There is one old dog called Biscuit. He came to the shelter five months ago. His owner had died, and nobody in the family wanted him. He was very thin and didn't trust anyone. I sat near his cage every Saturday and didn't try to touch him. After three weeks, he came to the door by himself. After two months, he let me stroke his head. Last Saturday, he placed his paw on my hand for the first time. I almost cried. He is too old for most people to adopt, so he might stay at the shelter. But he is no longer the same dog who arrived. Sometimes, just being there counts as helping.

annotations.exampleEn: She adopted a kitten from the animal shelter.
There are many young volunteers at the event.

annotations.phrase: lift the big bags of food
annotations.meaningVi: nhấc những bao thức ăn lớn
annotations.exampleEn: He can lift the big bags of food easily.
Many people walk dogs in this park.
The dog barked very loudly at the postman.
He was the most scared person in the room.
After the accident, he didn't trust anyone.
She let me stroke her cat's head.

annotations.phrase: placed his paw on my hand
annotations.exampleEn: The cat placed her paw on my hand and looked at me.
We hope someone will adopt this puppy.

annotations.phrase: just being there counts as helping
annotations.meaningVi: chỉ cần có mặt cũng đã là một sự giúp đỡ
annotations.exampleEn: For her grandmother, just being there counts as helping.

grammarNotes.title: Modal 'can' for ability
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng 'can + V' để nói khả năng làm việc gì; 'can't' / 'cannot' cho phủ định. Trong bài: 'I can walk dogs, clean the cat room, and fold the small towels for the puppies.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'can + verb' for ability. From the text: 'I can walk dogs, clean the cat room, and fold the small towels for the puppies.'

grammarNotes.title: 'too + adjective' for excess as a reason
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng 'too + tính từ' để nói điều gì đó vượt quá mức cho phép. Trong bài: 'He is too old for most people to adopt' — quá già để hầu hết người ta nhận nuôi.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'too + adjective' to say something is more than acceptable. From the text: 'He is too old for most people to adopt.'

translationVi: Tôi bắt đầu làm tình nguyện ở một trại cứu hộ động vật nhỏ gần cảng Sydney khoảng tám tháng trước. Mỗi sáng thứ Bảy tôi đến, từ tám giờ đến trưa. Thường có ba bốn tình nguyện viên khác, và chúng tôi chia nhau công việc. Tôi không đủ khoẻ để nhấc bao thức ăn lớn, nhưng tôi có thể dắt chó đi dạo, dọn phòng mèo và gấp những chiếc khăn nhỏ cho lũ cún. Lần đầu, tôi hơi sợ vì có vài con chó sủa rất to. Sau vài tuần, tôi hiểu rằng những con sủa to thường là sợ nhất, chứ không phải hung dữ nhất. Có một con chó già tên Biscuit. Nó đến trại năm tháng trước. Chủ của nó qua đời, và không ai trong gia đình muốn nuôi. Nó rất gầy và không tin ai. Tôi ngồi gần lồng nó mỗi thứ Bảy, không cố chạm vào. Ba tuần sau, nó tự ra phía cửa. Hai tháng sau, nó để tôi vuốt đầu. Thứ Bảy tuần trước, lần đầu tiên nó đặt chân lên tay tôi. Tôi suýt khóc. Nó quá già để hầu hết người ta nhận nuôi, nên có thể nó sẽ ở lại trại. Nhưng nó không còn là con chó như lúc mới đến. Đôi khi, chỉ cần có mặt thôi đã là sự giúp đỡ rồi.

options: At a small animal shelter near Sydney harbour
questions.explanation: 'I started volunteering at a small animal shelter near Sydney harbour.'
questions.hint: Listen for both the type and the location.

options: Every Saturday morning, from eight until noon
questions.explanation: 'I go every Saturday morning, from eight until noon.'
questions.hint: Listen for the day and the exact hours.

questions.prompt: What CAN'T she do at the shelter?
options: Lift the big bags of food
questions.explanation: 'I am not strong enough to lift the big bags of food.'
questions.hint: Listen for what is too heavy for her.

questions.prompt: What did she learn about dogs that bark loudly?
options: They are always the most dangerous
They are often the most scared, not the most dangerous
questions.explanation: 'loud dogs are often the most scared, not the most dangerous.'
questions.hint: Listen for a lesson she learned.

options: An old dog at the shelter
questions.explanation: 'There is one old dog called Biscuit.'
questions.hint: Listen for Biscuit's age and where he is.

questions.prompt: Why did Biscuit come to the shelter?
options: He was found in the street
His owner had died and the family didn't want him
He was too noisy at home
questions.explanation: 'His owner had died, and nobody in the family wanted him.'

questions.prompt: What did the narrator do every Saturday at the start, with Biscuit?
options: Tried to feed him by hand
Sat near his cage without trying to touch him
questions.explanation: 'I sat near his cage every Saturday and didn't try to touch him.'
questions.hint: Listen for her patient, quiet method.

questions.prompt: After how long did Biscuit let her stroke his head?
questions.explanation: 'After two months, he let me stroke his head.'
questions.hint: Listen for a length of time.

questions.prompt: What did Biscuit do for the first time last Saturday?
options: Placed his paw on her hand
questions.explanation: 'he placed his paw on my hand for the first time.'
questions.hint: Listen for the most recent step in their bond.

questions.prompt: Why might Biscuit stay at the shelter?
options: He is too old for most people to adopt
His old owner is coming back
questions.explanation: 'He is too old for most people to adopt, so he might stay at the shelter.'

questions.prompt: What is the main message at the end of the passage?
options: Sometimes, just being there is a kind of help
questions.explanation: 'Sometimes, just being there counts as helping.'
questions.hint: Listen for the moral at the very end.

cloze.template: I started volunteering at a small animal {{b1}} near Sydney harbour about eight months ago. I go every Saturday morning, from eight until noon. There are usually three or four other volunteers, and we share the work. I am not strong enough to {{b2}} the big bags of food, but I can walk dogs, clean the cat room, and fold the small towels for the puppies. The first time, I was a little afraid because some dogs barked very {{b3}}. After a few weeks, I learned that loud dogs are often the most scared, not the most {{b4}}. There is one old dog called {{b5}}. He came to the shelter five months ago. His owner had died, and nobody in the family wanted him. He was very {{b6}} and didn't trust anyone. I sat near his cage every Saturday and didn't try to {{b7}} him. After three weeks, he came to the door by himself. After two months, he let me stroke his head. Last Saturday, he placed his {{b8}} on my hand for the first time. I almost cried. He is too {{b9}} for most people to adopt, so he might stay at the shelter. But he is no longer the same dog who arrived. Sometimes, just being there counts as {{b10}}.

blanks.explanation: 'shelter' — a place that takes in animals without homes.
'loudly' — adverb describing how the dogs barked.
'dangerous' — the lesson is that loud doesn't mean dangerous.
'thin' — Biscuit was very thin when he arrived.
'touch' — she avoided physical contact at first.
'old' — Biscuit's age is too much for most adopters.
'helping' — the main idea of the passage.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator says that sitting quietly near Biscuit's cage was itself a kind of help. In your own life, when has someone helped you simply by being there — without giving advice or fixing anything? Why do you think presence alone can matter so much?
